- Each fallback attempt uses its own credentials (you might fall back from Anthropic to OpenAI, which requires different API keys).
- The platform tracks which model ultimately served the request, so you can see fallback usage in analytics.
- Fallback is transparent to the agent -- it receives a response regardless of which model served it.

To configure fallback models:

1. Go to **Settings > AI Configuration > Models**.
2. Select a registered model.
3. In the **Fallback** section, add one or more fallback models in priority order.
4. Click **Save**.

You can also declare fallback models directly in your agent definition:

```abl
AGENT CustomerSupport
  DESCRIPTION "Handles customer inquiries"
  MODEL "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4"
  FALLBACK_MODELS ["openai/gpt-4o", "google/gemini-2.5-flash"]
```

chunks.heading: Cost and token tracking
chunks.text:
### Cost and token tracking

Every LLM call tracks token usage and estimated cost. This data flows into the platform's analytics pipeline, where you can see it broken down by agent, project, model, and time period.

| Metric             | Description                                                                        |
| ------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Input tokens**   | Tokens sent to the model (system prompt + conversation history + tool definitions) |
| **Output tokens**  | Tokens generated by the model (response text + tool calls)                         |
| **Estimated cost** | Calculated from token counts and the model's known pricing                         |
| **Model used**     | Which model served the request (important when fallbacks are in play)              |
| **Latency**        | Time from request to first token (time-to-first-token) and total request duration  |

The platform maintains a catalog of known model pricing. For custom or self-hosted models where pricing is not known, token counts are still tracked -- you can apply your own cost formulas.

chunks.heading: Context window management
chunks.text:
### Context window management

Large conversations accumulate tokens. The Runtime automatically manages the context window to stay within model limits and control costs:

- **Tool result compression** -- Large tool results are compressed before being added to the conversation.
- **Prior turn truncation** -- Tool results from previous conversation turns are replaced with short placeholders.
- **Conversation compaction** -- When the conversation history grows beyond a threshold, older messages are summarized to reduce token usage while preserving important context.

chunks.heading: Supported Providers
chunks.text:
## Supported Providers

| Provider             | Auth type                     | Models                                             | Key strengths                                                  |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Anthropic**        | API key                       | Claude 4 Opus, Claude 4 Sonnet, Claude 3.5 Haiku   | Strong reasoning, long context (200K tokens), tool use, vision |
| **OpenAI**           | API key                       | GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, GPT-4o mini, o3, o4-mini          | Broad capabilities, vision, function calling                   |
| **Azure OpenAI**     | API key + endpoint            | GPT-4, GPT-4o (Azure deployments)                  | Enterprise compliance, regional deployment                     |
| **Google**           | API key                       | Gemini 2.5 Pro, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 2.0 Flash | Very large context windows (1M+ tokens), fast inference        |
| **Amazon Bedrock**   | IAM credentials               | Claude, Titan, Bedrock-hosted models               | AWS integration, VPC-private access                            |
| **Custom / LiteLLM** | API key, Bearer, or OAuth 2.0 | Any OpenAI-compatible endpoint                     | Unified interface to 100+ model providers                      |

Each provider requires its own credentials (API keys or cloud-native authentication). You configure credentials at the workspace level, and they are encrypted at rest.

chunks.heading: Add Provider Credentials
chunks.text:
## Add Provider Credentials

Before registering models, add your provider's API credentials:

1. Go to **Settings > AI Configuration > LLM Providers**.
2. Click **Add credential**.
3. Select the provider from the dropdown.
4. Enter a display name (for example, "Production Anthropic Key").
5. Enter the API key and, for Azure or custom providers, the endpoint URL.
6. Select the credential scope:
   - **Workspace** -- Available to all projects and members in the workspace.
   - **Personal** -- Available only to you, useful for experimentation.
7. Click **Validate and save**.

The platform validates credentials by making a test API call. If validation fails:

- Confirm the API key is correct and active.
- Confirm the endpoint URL is correct (Azure, custom, and LiteLLM).
- Confirm your network allows outbound HTTPS to the provider.

> **Security:** API keys are encrypted at rest using the workspace encryption key and never displayed in plaintext after initial entry. See [Workspace Configuration](./workspace-configuration) for security details.

### Credential lifecycle

Each credential tracks:

- **Active/Inactive status** -- Deactivate during key rotation without deleting.
- **Last used** -- Timestamp of the most recent API call.
- **Last validated** -- Timestamp of the most recent successful validation.

chunks.heading: Model Tiers & Defaults
chunks.text:
## Model Tiers & Defaults

Model tiers decouple agent definitions from specific model choices. Agents reference tiers, and the platform resolves the tier to a specific model at runtime.

| Tier         | Intended use                                     | Typical models                                  |
| ------------ | ------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------- |
| **Fast**     | Classification, routing, quick responses         | Claude 3.5 Haiku, GPT-4o mini, Gemini 2.0 Flash |
| **Balanced** | General-purpose tasks balancing quality and cost | Claude 4 Sonnet, GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Flash       |
| **Powerful** | Complex reasoning, analysis, content generation  | Claude 4 Opus, o3, Gemini 2.5 Pro               |
| **Voice**    | Real-time voice interaction                      | GPT-4o Realtime, Gemini 2.0 Flash (Live)        |

### Setting workspace defaults

1. Go to **Settings > AI Configuration > LLM Providers**.
2. In the **Default models** section, select a model for each tier dropdown.
3. Click **Save defaults**.

Defaults apply to all new projects. Existing projects retain their assignments unless updated.

### Choosing the right model

Selecting a model is a trade-off between three dimensions: quality, speed, and cost.

- **Start with a balanced model.** Most agents perform well with a balanced-tier model. Only move to a more powerful (or faster) model if you identify a specific need.
- **Use fast models for internal operations.** Entity extraction, field validation, and summarization often do not need the most powerful model.
- **Use powerful models for complex reasoning.** If your agent needs to make nuanced decisions or synthesize information from multiple sources, a powerful model produces better results.
- **Match the model to the context window.** If your agent handles long documents, choose a model with a large context window. Gemini models offer 1M+ token contexts; Claude models offer 200K tokens.
- **Consider latency for real-time interactions.** Voice agents and real-time chat need fast time-to-first-token.
- **Test with real conversations.** The best way to choose a model is to test your agent with representative conversations and compare quality, speed, and cost.

chunks.heading: Register Custom Models
chunks.text:
## Register Custom Models

To make a specific model available in your workspace:

1. Go to **Settings > AI Configuration > Models**.
2. Click **Add model**.
3. Select the provider and model from the catalog (or enter a custom model ID for LiteLLM/custom endpoints).
4. Assign the model to a tier (**Fast**, **Balanced**, **Powerful**, or **Voice**).
5. Associate a credential from the ones you registered.
6. Set default hyperparameters:

| Parameter             | Default | Description                                                           |
| --------------------- | ------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Temperature**       | 0.7     | Controls randomness. Lower values produce more deterministic outputs. |
| **Max tokens**        | 4096    | Maximum number of tokens the model can generate in a response.        |
| **Top P**             | 1.0     | Nucleus sampling threshold.                                           |
| **Frequency penalty** | 0.0     | Reduces repetition of token sequences.                                |
| **Presence penalty**  | 0.0     | Encourages the model to discuss new topics.                           |
| **Timeout (ms)**      | 30000   | Request timeout in milliseconds.                                      |

7. Mark capabilities:

| Capability         | Description                                       |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------- |
| **Text**           | Standard text generation (all models)             |
| **Tools**          | Function/tool calling support                     |
| **Streaming**      | Server-sent events streaming for real-time output |
| **Vision**         | Image input processing                            |
| **Realtime voice** | WebSocket-based real-time voice interaction       |

The platform uses these flags to validate agent configurations. If an agent requires tool calling but the assigned model does not support it, a validation warning is shown.

8. Click **Save**.

You can adjust hyperparameters per model registration. Individual agents can also override hyperparameters at the agent level.

chunks.heading: Project-Level Overrides
chunks.text:
## Project-Level Overrides

Projects inherit the workspace's model configuration but can customize how tiers map to operations.

### Operation tier overrides

1. Go to **Project > Settings > LLM Configuration**.
2. For each operation, select the tier override from the dropdown.
3. Click **Save**.

| Operation        | Default tier | Description                             |
| ---------------- | ------------ | --------------------------------------- |
| `extraction`     | Fast         | Parsing structured data from user input |
| `validation`     | Fast         | Input validation and format checking    |
| `tool_selection` | Fast         | Choosing which tool to invoke           |
| `response_gen`   | Balanced     | Generating user-facing responses        |
| `summarization`  | Balanced     | Summarizing conversations or documents  |
| `reasoning`      | Powerful     | Multi-step reasoning and analysis       |
| `coordination`   | Powerful     | Supervisor routing and orchestration    |
| `realtime_voice` | Voice        | Real-time voice interactions            |

These overrides only affect the current project. Other projects continue using the workspace defaults.

### Allowing project credentials

By default, projects use workspace-level LLM credentials. To allow individual projects to register their own credentials (for example, to use a dedicated API key with separate billing):

1. Go to **Settings > AI Configuration > LLM Providers**.
2. Toggle **Allow project-level credentials** to enabled.

When enabled, project Admins can add provider credentials scoped to their project.

chunks.heading: Provider-Specific Configuration
chunks.text:
## Provider-Specific Configuration

### Anthropic

- **Base URL:** `https://api.anthropic.com` (default, configurable for proxy setups)
- **API version:** `2023-06-01` (default)
- **Extended thinking:** Enable per-project for models that support it

### OpenAI

- **Standard and Azure endpoints** are treated as separate providers.
- For Azure, you need the deployment URL, API key, and API version.
- Reasoning models (o3, o4-mini) support a `reasoningEffort` parameter (`low`, `medium`, `high`).

### Google Gemini

- Long-context models (Gemini 2.5 Pro: up to 1M tokens) are available for document-heavy workflows.
- Voice-capable models (Gemini 2.0 Flash) support the **Voice** tier for real-time interactions.

### Amazon Bedrock

- Provide IAM access key and secret key (or configure an IAM role for the runtime).
- Model IDs use the Bedrock format (for example, `anthropic.claude-v2`).

### LiteLLM / custom endpoints

- Set the proxy URL in workspace settings.
- Model IDs use the LiteLLM format (for example, `openai/gpt-4o` or `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4`).
- The platform routes requests through the LiteLLM proxy, which handles provider normalization.

chunks.heading: LLM Policies
chunks.text:
## LLM Policies

Workspace-level LLM policies govern usage limits and provider restrictions:

| Setting                      | Description                                                                   |
| ---------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Allowed providers**        | Restrict which LLM providers can be used in this workspace                    |
| **Model allowlist**          | Restrict specific models that can be used                                     |
| **Monthly token budget**     | Maximum tokens across all models for the billing month                        |
| **Daily token budget**       | Maximum tokens per day (prevents runaway costs)                               |
| **Max requests per minute**  | Rate limit on LLM API calls from this workspace                               |
| **Token budget per project** | Set monthly token limits per project or per user                              |
| **Platform demo mode**       | Enable demo mode with platform-provided credentials (for evaluation purposes) |

To configure these settings:

1. Go to **Settings > AI Configuration > LLM Providers**.
2. Click **LLM Policy**.
3. Adjust the settings as needed.
4. Click **Save**.

> **Tip:** Set daily token budgets conservatively during initial rollout. You can increase them as you understand your agents' actual consumption patterns. Monitor usage in [Billing, Quotas & Operations](./billing-quotas-and-operations).

chunks.heading: Troubleshooting
chunks.text:
## Troubleshooting

### Model validation fails

- Confirm the API key is valid by testing it directly with the provider's API.
- For Azure deployments, verify the endpoint URL includes the deployment name.
- Check that your workspace's network (or proxy configuration) allows outbound HTTPS connections to the provider.

### Agent cannot find a model for the requested tier

- Verify that at least one active model is assigned to the tier the agent is requesting.
- Check that the model's credential is active and not expired.

### Token budget exceeded

- Review current consumption in **Settings > Account > Billing**.
- Increase the daily or monthly budget in the LLM Policy settings.
- Consider switching high-volume operations to a Fast-tier model to reduce token usage.

chunks.heading: untitled
chunks.text:
# Billing, Quotas & Operations

Monitor your workspace's resource consumption, manage your plan, and operate the Agent Platform 2.0 at scale. This page covers billing and usage tracking, platform limits, customer-managed encryption keys, analytics, self-hosted deployment, and upgrade procedures.

chunks.heading: Billing & Usage
chunks.text:
## Billing & Usage

**Required role:** Owner or Admin to manage billing; Operator to view usage

chunks.heading: Plan tiers
chunks.text:
### Plan tiers

The Agent Platform 2.0 offers three plan tiers:

|                        | Starter | Professional  | Enterprise    |
| ---------------------- | ------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| **Team members**       | Up to 5 | Up to 25      | Unlimited     |
| **Projects**           | Up to 3 | Up to 20      | Unlimited     |
| **Monthly sessions**   | 1,000   | 50,000        | Custom        |
| **Monthly tokens**     | 1M      | 50M           | Custom        |
| **LLM providers**      | 2       | All supported | All supported |
| **Connectors**         | --      | Yes           | Yes           |
| **Advanced analytics** | --      | Yes           | Yes           |
| **Guardrails**         | --      | Yes           | Yes           |
| **SSO**                | --      | --            | Yes           |
| **KMS (BYOK)**         | --      | --            | Yes           |
| **Dedicated support**  | --      | --            | Yes           |
| **SLA**                | --      | 99.9%         | 99.95%        |

chunks.heading: Viewing your current plan
chunks.text:
### Viewing your current plan

Go to **Settings > Account > Billing**. The top of the page shows your current plan, billing cycle, and renewal date.

chunks.heading: Credit balance
chunks.text:
### Credit balance

If your workspace uses a credit-based billing model, the billing page displays:

- **Total allocated credits** -- Credits available for the current billing period
- **Credits consumed** -- How many credits have been used
- **Remaining credits** -- Available balance
- **Credit burn rate** -- Average daily consumption for the period

Credits are consumed by LLM API calls, session activity, and feature usage. The credit cost varies by model tier and operation type.

#### Low credit alerts

Set up alerts to be notified when credits are running low:

1. Go to **Settings > Account > Billing**.
2. In the **Alerts** section, click **Add alert**.
3. Configure the alert:
   - **Type** -- Select "Credit low"
   - **Threshold** -- The credit percentage at which to trigger (for example, 20% remaining)
   - **Channel** -- Email or webhook
   - **Target** -- The email address or webhook URL to notify
4. Click **Save**.

chunks.heading: LLM usage analytics
chunks.text:
### LLM usage analytics

The billing page includes detailed analytics on LLM consumption:

**Summary metrics** displayed at the top:

- **Total sessions** -- Number of agent sessions in the selected period
- **Total tokens** -- Input and output tokens consumed across all models
- **Total estimated cost** -- Calculated cost based on model pricing
- **Active members** -- Team members who triggered LLM calls

**Breakdowns available:**

- **Daily usage trends** -- Time-series chart of token consumption. Use it to identify usage spikes, spot trends, and verify that usage drops after decommissioning agents.
- **By provider** -- Pie chart showing token distribution across LLM providers.
- **By model** -- Input tokens, output tokens, average latency, error rate, and estimated cost per model.
- **By agent** -- Identifies agents that could benefit from a lower-cost tier, have unexpectedly high token usage, or are inactive.

chunks.heading: Usage thresholds and alerts
chunks.text:
### Usage thresholds and alerts

Configure alerts to prevent cost overruns:

- **Usage threshold alerts** -- Notify when a percentage of the monthly token budget is reached (e.g., 80%).
- **Health degraded alerts** -- Notify when LLM provider connections experience degraded performance.
- **Feature limit alerts** -- Notify when approaching plan limits for sessions, members, or projects.

Configure alerts at **Settings > Account > Billing > Alerts**.

chunks.heading: Billing periods
chunks.text:
### Billing periods

Usage is tracked in billing periods that align with your subscription cycle:

- **Monthly billing** -- Usage resets on your billing anniversary date.
- **Annual billing** -- Monthly usage is tracked within the annual period for budgeting, but limits are enforced monthly.

Each billing period records total sessions, messages, tokens, tool calls, estimated cost, peak concurrent sessions, and invoice status.

chunks.heading: Plan upgrades
chunks.text:
### Plan upgrades

To upgrade your plan:

1. Go to **Settings > Account > Billing**.
2. Click **Upgrade plan** or **Compare plans**.
3. Review the available tiers and their features.
4. Select the plan you want.
5. Confirm the upgrade.

Plan upgrades take effect immediately. The billing difference is prorated for the remainder of the current billing period.

> **Note:** Plan downgrades take effect at the end of the current billing period. Features exclusive to your current plan remain accessible until the period ends.

chunks.heading: Cost optimization tips
chunks.text:
### Cost optimization tips

- **Use the right tier for the job.** Route simple classification tasks to Fast-tier models instead of Powerful-tier models. The cost difference can be 10x or more.
- **Set daily token budgets.** Even if your monthly budget is generous, a daily limit prevents a single runaway agent from consuming a month's worth of tokens in a day.
- **Review agent-level usage monthly.** Identify agents with high per-session token counts and consider whether their prompts can be optimized or their context windows reduced.
- **Enable conversation sliding windows.** For long-running agents, sliding windows limit the conversation history sent to the LLM, reducing input token costs.
- **Archive inactive projects.** Inactive projects with deployed agents may still consume resources from scheduled tasks or webhook-triggered sessions.

chunks.heading: Platform Limits & Quotas
chunks.text:
## Platform Limits & Quotas

Every workspace operates within limits that protect platform stability and ensure fair resource allocation. Limits vary by plan tier and can be adjusted for Enterprise customers.

chunks.heading: Capacity limits by plan
chunks.text:
### Capacity limits by plan

| Capability             | Starter | Professional | Enterprise |
| ---------------------- | ------- | ------------ | ---------- |
| Concurrent sessions    | 10      | 1,000        | Unlimited  |
| Projects per workspace | 3       | 20           | Unlimited  |
| Agents per project     | 5       | 100          | Unlimited  |
| Messages per month     | 1,000   | 500,000      | Unlimited  |
| Requests per minute    | 20      | 200          | Custom     |
| Tokens per minute      | 10,000  | 500,000      | Custom     |
| Tool calls per minute  | 20      | 500          | Unlimited  |

chunks.heading: Rate limits
chunks.text:
### Rate limits

Rate limits protect the platform from abuse and ensure consistent performance.

| Endpoint category     | Window      | Default max       | Notes                          |
| --------------------- | ----------- | ----------------- | ------------------------------ |
| Authentication routes | 15 minutes  | 20                | Login, token refresh           |
| API routes            | 1 minute    | 100               | General API calls              |
| Chat messages         | Per session | Configurable      | Tenant-level override          |
| Webhook inbound       | Per channel | No platform limit | External platform limits apply |

When you exceed a rate limit, the API returns `429 Too Many Requests` with a `Retry-After` header.

Token limits apply to LLM API calls and are tracked per workspace:

| Metric                   | Tracked per | Default    |
| ------------------------ | ----------- | ---------- |
| Input tokens per minute  | Workspace   | 100,000    |
| Output tokens per minute | Workspace   | 100,000    |
| Total tokens per month   | Workspace   | Plan-based |

chunks.heading: Payload limits
chunks.text:
### Payload limits

| Resource                 | Limit        | Notes                                   |
| ------------------------ | ------------ | --------------------------------------- |
| Request body size        | 10 MB        | JSON and multipart                      |
| Webhook payload          | 1 MB         | Inbound from external platforms         |
| File upload (attachment) | 25 MB        | Per file                                |
| Batch upload             | 100 MB       | Total per request                       |
| Response body size       | Configurable | `maxResponseBodyBytes` in tenant config |

chunks.heading: Session limits
chunks.text:
### Session limits

| Parameter                   | Default     | Configurable | Description                                |
| --------------------------- | ----------- | ------------ | ------------------------------------------ |
| `maxConcurrentSessions`     | Plan-based  | Yes          | Active sessions across all projects        |
| `sessionMaxAgeSeconds`      | 86400 (24h) | Yes          | Maximum session duration                   |
| `sessionIdleSeconds`        | 1800 (30m)  | Yes          | Inactivity timeout before session expires  |
| `maxServiceTimeoutMs`       | 30000 (30s) | Yes          | Maximum timeout for external service calls |
| `maxConcurrentServiceCalls` | 10          | Yes          | Parallel external calls per session        |
| `maxPendingTimers`          | 50          | Yes          | Maximum scheduled timers per session       |

chunks.heading: Knowledge base limits
chunks.text:
### Knowledge base limits

| Resource                     | Starter | Professional | Enterprise |
| ---------------------------- | ------- | ------------ | ---------- |
| Knowledge bases per project  | 1       | 20           | Unlimited  |
| Documents per knowledge base | 100     | 10,000       | Unlimited  |
| Document size                | 10 MB   | 100 MB       | 200 MB     |
| Total storage per workspace  | 1 GB    | 100 GB       | Custom     |

chunks.heading: Data retention
chunks.text:
### Data retention

| Data type      | Default retention | Configurable            |
| -------------- | ----------------- | ----------------------- |
| Session data   | 30 days           | `sessionRetentionDays`  |
| Messages       | 90 days           | `messageRetentionDays`  |
| Trace data     | 30 days           | `traceRetentionDays`    |
| Audit logs     | 90 days           | `auditLogRetentionDays` |
| Analytics data | 90 days           | Plan-based              |

Enterprise plans support custom retention periods. Data is automatically deleted after the retention period via TTL indexes.

chunks.heading: Channel-specific limits
chunks.text:
### Channel-specific limits

| Channel         | Message size      | Media size                   | Rate limit                  |
| --------------- | ----------------- | ---------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| Slack           | 40,000 chars      | 1 GB                         | Platform rate (tier 3)      |
| Microsoft Teams | 28 KB             | 10 MB                        | 2 msgs/sec per conversation |
| WhatsApp        | 4,096 chars       | 16 MB (media), 100 MB (docs) | Per Meta policies           |
| Telegram        | 4,096 chars       | 50 MB                        | 30 msgs/sec                 |
| Email           | No platform limit | 25 MB (attachments)          | SMTP provider limits        |
| Web SDK         | No platform limit | 25 MB (uploads)              | Workspace rate limit        |

chunks.heading: Security limits
chunks.text:
### Security limits

| Parameter               | Default    | Description                                    |
| ----------------------- | ---------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| `allowedServiceDomains` | `[]` (all) | Restrict outbound tool calls to listed domains |
| `requireMtls`           | `false`    | Require mutual TLS for service calls           |
| `ipAllowlist`           | `[]` (all) | Restrict API access to listed IP ranges        |
| `requireMfa`            | `false`    | Require MFA for all workspace members          |
| `apiKeyMaxAgeDays`      | 90         | Maximum age before API key rotation required   |
| `scrubPII`              | `false`    | Scrub PII from traces and analytics            |

chunks.heading: Monitoring limits
chunks.text:
### Monitoring limits

Check your current usage against limits:

1. Go to **Settings > Usage**.
2. View real-time metrics for sessions, messages, tokens, and storage.
3. Configure alerts for approaching limits at **Settings > Alerts**.

**Requesting limit increases:**

- **Professional plan:** Contact your account manager or submit a request at **Settings > Usage > Request increase**.
- **Enterprise plan:** Limits are configured during onboarding and can be adjusted at any time through your account team.

chunks.heading: KMS (Bring Your Own Key)
chunks.text:
## KMS (Bring Your Own Key)

The Key Management Service (KMS) feature lets you control the encryption keys used to protect your workspace's sensitive data. Instead of relying on platform-managed keys, you provide your own key material from a supported cloud provider.

**Required role:** Owner

> **Plan requirement:** KMS is available on Enterprise plans.

chunks.heading: Why bring your own key
chunks.text:
### Why bring your own key

By default, the Agent Platform 2.0 encrypts all sensitive data using platform-managed encryption keys. Bring Your Own Key (BYOK) gives you:

- **Key custody** -- You own and control the master encryption key. You can revoke it at any time.
- **Compliance** -- Meet regulatory requirements that mandate customer-managed encryption keys (SOC 2, HIPAA, FedRAMP).
- **Key rotation control** -- Rotate keys on your schedule, according to your organization's cryptographic policies.
- **Audit trail** -- Your cloud provider logs every key usage event, giving you an independent audit trail.

chunks.heading: Encryption architecture
chunks.text:
### Encryption architecture

The platform uses an envelope encryption model:

1. **Key Encryption Key (KEK)** -- Your master key, stored in your cloud provider's key management service. The platform never has access to the raw key material.
2. **Data Encryption Keys (DEKs)** -- Short-lived keys generated by the platform to encrypt individual data items. Each DEK is wrapped (encrypted) by your KEK before storage.
3. **Encryption at rest** -- When the platform encrypts a secret, it generates a DEK, encrypts the data, wraps the DEK with your KEK, and stores both. Decryption reverses the process.

DEKs are scoped to a specific project and environment, with configurable rotation intervals.

chunks.heading: Supported KMS providers
chunks.text:
### Supported KMS providers

| Provider             | Key type                | Region support      |
| -------------------- | ----------------------- | ------------------- |
| **AWS KMS**          | Symmetric CMK (AES-256) | All AWS regions     |
| **Azure Key Vault**  | RSA or AES keys         | All Azure regions   |
| **Google Cloud KMS** | Symmetric AES-256 keys  | All GCP regions     |
| **External KMS**     | Any REST-compatible KMS | Self-hosted or SaaS |

chunks.heading: Setting up KMS
chunks.text:
### Setting up KMS

**Step 1: Create a key in your provider**

Create a symmetric encryption key in your cloud provider's KMS:

- **AWS KMS:** Create a Customer Managed Key (CMK). Note the Key ID and region. Create an IAM policy granting `kms:Encrypt`, `kms:Decrypt`, `kms:GenerateDataKey`, and `kms:DescribeKey` permissions.
- **Azure Key Vault:** Create a Key Vault and generate a key. Note the Key Vault URL and key name. Grant the Agent Platform 2.0 service principal the "Key Vault Crypto User" role.
- **Google Cloud KMS:** Create a key ring and key. Note the resource name. Grant the Agent Platform 2.0 service account the `cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter` role.

**Step 2: Configure KMS in the Agent Platform 2.0**

1. Go to **Settings > Team > KMS**.
2. Click **Configure KMS**.
3. Select your provider.
4. Enter the key details:
   - **Key ID** -- The identifier for your key (ARN for AWS, URL for Azure, resource name for GCP)
   - **Region** -- The region where the key is hosted
   - **Authentication** -- Service account credentials or role-based access configuration
5. Click **Validate connection**.
6. If validation succeeds, click **Enable KMS**.

**Step 3: Migration**

When you enable KMS for the first time, the platform re-encrypts existing secrets and credentials using your key. Existing DEKs are re-wrapped with your new KEK. New encryption operations use your key immediately. The migration status is visible on the KMS settings page.

chunks.heading: Key rotation
chunks.text:
### Key rotation

Rotate your encryption key periodically to limit the impact of key compromise.

**Automatic rotation:** If your cloud provider supports automatic key rotation (AWS KMS and GCP KMS do), enable it in your provider's console. The platform automatically detects the new key version.

**Manual rotation:**

1. Create a new key version in your cloud provider.
2. Go to **Settings > Team > KMS**.
3. Click **Rotate key**.
4. Enter the new key version details.
5. Click **Initiate rotation**.

The platform starts using the new key version for all new encryption operations, re-wraps existing DEKs in the background, and marks the old key version as "decrypt only" once complete.

> **Important:** Do not destroy the old key version in your cloud provider until the Agent Platform 2.0 shows all data has been re-encrypted with the new version. Destroying a key version while data still depends on it results in permanent data loss.

chunks.heading: KMS health monitoring
chunks.text:
### KMS health monitoring

The platform periodically verifies connectivity to your KMS provider. If the health check fails, the platform follows the configured failure policy:

- **Fail-closed** -- All encryption and decryption operations fail. Agents cannot access secrets. Safest option but disrupts service.
- **Graceful degradation** -- The platform continues operating with cached DEKs until the KMS connection is restored.

Configure the failure policy at **Settings > Team > KMS > Failure policy**.

chunks.heading: Disabling KMS
chunks.text:
### Disabling KMS

To revert to platform-managed encryption:

1. Go to **Settings > Team > KMS**.
2. Click **Disable KMS**.
3. Confirm the action.

The platform re-encrypts all data using platform-managed keys. Your cloud KMS key is no longer accessed after migration completes.

chunks.heading: Advanced Analytics
chunks.text:
## Advanced Analytics

Advanced analytics provide deep visibility into agent performance, user sessions, and execution traces.

**Required role:** Owner, Admin, or Operator to view analytics; Member with project access for project-scoped views

> **Plan requirement:** Advanced analytics are available on Professional and Enterprise plans.

### Agent performance dashboard

The agent performance dashboard shows aggregate metrics:

| Metric                        | Description                                              |
| ----------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Total sessions**            | Number of agent sessions in the selected time range      |
| **Avg. session duration**     | Mean time from session start to last message             |
| **Resolution rate**           | Percentage of sessions that reached a successful outcome |
| **Avg. messages per session** | Mean number of user and agent messages per session       |
| **Total tokens consumed**     | Input + output tokens across all models                  |
| **Total estimated cost**      | Calculated LLM cost for the period                       |
| **Error rate**                | Percentage of sessions that encountered errors           |

Filter by date range, project, agent, or model. Charts include session volume over time, token usage, cost trends, top agents by session count and cost, and error breakdown.

### Session explorer

Browse and search individual sessions to understand user interactions and agent behavior.

1. Go to **Settings > Analytics > Sessions**.
2. Search by session ID, user message content, or agent name. Filter by status, date range, or agent.
3. Click a session to see the conversation transcript, tool calls, model usage, state transitions, and metadata.

### Trace viewer

The trace viewer provides the deepest level of detail, showing the execution trace of a single interaction.

Each trace records a **span tree** -- a hierarchical view of all operations:

- LLM calls (prompt, response, tokens, latency)
- Tool executions (name, input, output, duration)
- Step transitions (for agents with a FLOW section)
- Guardrail evaluations (category, score, decision)
- Knowledge base queries (query text, retrieved chunks, relevance scores)

**Trace search syntax:**

| Operator     | Example                  | Description              |
| ------------ | ------------------------ | ------------------------ |
| `agent:`     | `agent:customer-support` | Filter by agent name     |
| `status:`    | `status:error`           | Filter by outcome        |
| `model:`     | `model:claude-4-sonnet`  | Filter by model used     |
| `duration:>` | `duration:>2s`           | Filter by total duration |
| `tokens:>`   | `tokens:>5000`           | Filter by token count    |

### Custom dimensions

Configure custom dimensions that are automatically extracted from session data:

1. Open the project in Studio.
2. Go to **Project settings > Advanced**.
3. In the **Trace dimensions** section, add session data keys (for example, `customer_tier`, `region`, `channel`).
4. Click **Save**.

Custom dimensions appear in analytics filters, letting you segment metrics by business-relevant attributes.

### Exporting analytics data

- **Session export:** Apply filters in the session explorer, click **Export**, and select CSV or JSON.
- **API access:** Use the Agent Platform 2.0 API with an API key that has the `analytics:read` scope for programmatic access.

chunks.heading: Self-Hosted Deployment
chunks.text:
## Self-Hosted Deployment

Deploy the Agent Platform 2.0 on your own infrastructure for full control over data residency, security, and scaling.

chunks.heading: System requirements
chunks.text:
### System requirements

**Minimum (development / small team):**

| Resource          | Specification                                    |
| ----------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| CPU               | 4 cores                                          |
| Memory            | 16 GB RAM                                        |
| Storage           | 50 GB SSD                                        |
| OS                | Linux (Ubuntu 22.04+, RHEL 8+), macOS (dev only) |
| Container runtime | Docker 24+ with Compose v2                       |
| Node.js           | 20 LTS (if running outside containers)           |

**Recommended (production):**

| Resource      | Specification                         |
| ------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| CPU           | 16+ cores                             |
| Memory        | 64+ GB RAM                            |
| Storage       | 500 GB+ NVMe SSD                      |
| Orchestrator  | Kubernetes 1.28+                      |
| Load balancer | Nginx, HAProxy, or cloud ALB          |
| TLS           | Certificates for all public endpoints |

chunks.heading: Service architecture
chunks.text:
### Service architecture

| Service              | Default port | Description                                        |
| -------------------- | ------------ | -------------------------------------------------- |
| **Runtime**          | 3112         | Agent execution engine, chat API, channel webhooks |
| **Studio**           | 5173         | Web-based development environment (Next.js)        |
| **Admin**            | 3003         | Platform administration API                        |
| **SearchAI**         | 3113         | Knowledge base ingestion and search                |
| **SearchAI Runtime** | 3114         | RAG query execution                                |

**Infrastructure dependencies:**

| Service        | Default port | Required                                                 |
| -------------- | ------------ | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| **MongoDB**    | 27018        | Yes -- primary database                                  |
| **Redis**      | 6380         | Yes -- session state, caching, distributed locks, BullMQ |
| **ClickHouse** | 8124         | Optional -- analytics and metrics                        |
| **Kafka**      | 19092        | Optional -- event streaming for analytics pipelines      |

**Optional Python services:**

| Service           | Default port | Description                          |
| ----------------- | ------------ | ------------------------------------ |
| **Docling**       | 8080         | Document parsing (PDF, DOCX, images) |
| **BGE-M3**        | 8000         | Embedding model for knowledge search |
| **Preprocessing** | 8003         | Text preprocessing and chunking      |

chunks.heading: Configuration reference
chunks.text:
### Configuration reference

Configuration is organized into schemas validated at startup. Invalid configuration causes the service to fail fast with a descriptive error. Key configuration categories:

- **Server:** `server.port`, `server.host`, `server.apiUrl`, `server.frontendUrl`
- **Database:** `database.url` (MongoDB connection string), `database.poolSize`
- **Redis:** `redis.url`, `redis.enabled`, TLS settings, cluster mode
- **JWT/Auth:** `jwt.secret` (32+ characters required), `jwt.expiresIn`, Google OAuth credentials
- **LLM:** Default provider, API keys per provider, `llm.defaultModel`, `llm.fastModel`, fallback chain, timeout, cache
- **Encryption:** `encryption.key` (256-bit, required for credential storage), `encryption.algorithm`
- **Security:** PII detection/redaction, rate limiting, IP allowlists
- **Observability:** Trace sampling, metrics, logging level, OTLP endpoint
- **Voice:** Twilio, Deepgram, ElevenLabs, LiveKit, Jambonz credentials
- **Sandbox:** Code execution timeout, Python/JavaScript pod URLs

chunks.heading: Database setup
chunks.text:
### Database setup

**MongoDB:** Deploy MongoDB 7.0+ as a replica set (required for change streams and transactions). The platform creates indexes and collections automatically on first startup.

**Redis:** Deploy Redis 7+ with persistence enabled (AOF recommended). For production, use Redis Sentinel or Redis Cluster for high availability. Enable TLS for production deployments.

**ClickHouse (optional):** Deploy for analytics if you need usage dashboards, conversation analytics, or custom metrics.

chunks.heading: Security checklist
chunks.text:
### Security checklist

Complete before exposing the platform to production traffic:

- TLS on all public endpoints
- 256-bit encryption key configured
- Strong JWT signing secret (32+ characters)
- MongoDB and Redis authentication enabled
- PII detection and redaction enabled
- Rate limiting enabled with appropriate limits
- Admin routes restricted to known IP ranges
- API key rotation configured (default 90 days)
- Outbound domain restriction for tool calls
- Audit logging enabled and exported to SIEM
- Secrets stored in a vault (not in environment variables)

chunks.heading: Vault providers
chunks.text:
### Vault providers

| Provider                  | Use case                                        |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| **Environment variables** | Development and simple deployments              |
| **File provider**         | Config files on disk                            |
| **HashiCorp Vault**       | Production -- centralized secrets with rotation |
| **AWS Secrets Manager**   | AWS-native deployments                          |
| **Azure Key Vault**       | Azure-native deployments                        |
| **Kubernetes Secrets**    | Kubernetes-native deployments                   |
| **Composite**             | Combine multiple providers with fallback        |

chunks.heading: Health checks
chunks.text:
### Health checks

| Service  | Endpoint      | Method | Healthy response                 |
| -------- | ------------- | ------ | -------------------------------- |
| Runtime  | `/health`     | GET    | `200 OK` with `{ status: "ok" }` |
| Studio   | `/api/health` | GET    | `200 OK`                         |
| Admin    | `/health`     | GET    | `200 OK`                         |
| SearchAI | `/health`     | GET    | `200 OK`                         |

chunks.heading: Scaling
chunks.text:
### Scaling

The Runtime and SearchAI services are stateless and can be horizontally scaled behind a load balancer. Session state lives in Redis, and all shared state is in MongoDB.

| Sessions        | Runtime replicas | Redis              | MongoDB            |
| --------------- | ---------------- | ------------------ | ------------------ |
| < 100           | 1 -- 2           | Single instance    | Single replica set |
| 100 -- 1,000    | 2 -- 4           | Sentinel (3 nodes) | 3-node replica set |
| 1,000 -- 10,000 | 4 -- 8           | Cluster (6 nodes)  | Sharded cluster    |
| 10,000+         | 8+               | Cluster (6+ nodes) | Sharded cluster    |

chunks.heading: Upgrade & Migration
chunks.text:
## Upgrade & Migration

Plan and execute platform upgrades with minimal downtime.

### Compatibility matrix

| Platform version | MongoDB | Redis | Node.js | Kubernetes | Breaking changes |
| ---------------- | ------- | ----- | ------- | ---------- | ---------------- |
| 1.x              | 7.0+    | 7.0+  | 20 LTS  | 1.28+      | --               |
| 0.9.x            | 6.0+    | 6.2+  | 20 LTS  | 1.27+      | --               |
| 0.8.x            | 6.0+    | 6.2+  | 18 LTS  | 1.26+      | Config schema v2 |

> **Upgrade path:** Always upgrade one major version at a time. Skipping versions is not supported.

### Pre-upgrade checklist

- Read release notes for every version between your current version and the target.
- Back up MongoDB (`mongodump` or filesystem snapshot) and Redis (`BGSAVE`).
- Verify sufficient disk space (2x current database size recommended).
- Confirm infrastructure meets the target version's requirements.
- Test the upgrade in a staging environment first.
- Schedule a maintenance window if the upgrade includes database migrations.
- Notify affected users of planned downtime.

### Upgrade procedures

**Docker Compose:**

```bash
docker compose down
docker compose pull
docker compose up -d
# Verify health
curl http://localhost:3112/health
curl http://localhost:5173/api/health
```

**Kubernetes (Helm):**

```bash
helm repo update
helm diff upgrade abl-platform abl-platform/abl-platform \
  -f values.yaml --version <target-version>
helm upgrade abl-platform abl-platform/abl-platform \
  -f values.yaml --version <target-version> --wait --timeout 10m
kubectl rollout status deployment/abl-runtime -n agent-platform
```

For zero-downtime rolling upgrades, configure `maxSurge: 1` and `maxUnavailable: 0`. New pods pass readiness checks before old pods terminate. WebSocket connections drain gracefully.

### Database migrations

The platform applies most schema changes automatically on startup (indexes, collection schemas, TTL indexes). For upgrades requiring manual intervention, the release notes include exact commands:

```bash
# Dry run
npx abl-migrate --target <version> --dry-run

# Apply
npx abl-migrate --target <version> --apply

# Verify
npx abl-migrate --target <version> --verify
```

### Breaking changes log

**Version 1.0:**

- Config schema v3: validated by Zod schemas at startup. Invalid configuration causes fail-fast.
- MongoDB required: SQLite/Prisma support removed.
- Redis required for production: session state and distributed locks require Redis.

**Version 0.9:**

- Encryption key must be 256-bit hex string.
- JWT secret must be at least 32 characters.

**Version 0.8:**

- Config schema v2: flat env vars moved to structured schemas. `env-mapping` utility provides backward compatibility.
- Rate limit configuration moved to `security.rateLimiting`.

### Configuration diff tool

Compare configuration between versions:

```bash
npx abl-config-diff --from <current-version> --to <target-version>
```

Outputs added, changed, and removed options plus validation warnings.

### Rollback procedures

**Docker Compose:** Stop services, restore previous image tags, restore database backups if needed, restart.

**Kubernetes:** `helm rollback abl-platform <previous-revision>`, restore database backup if needed.

### Post-upgrade verification

After upgrading, verify:

1. Health checks pass for all services.
2. Existing agents execute correctly (run a test conversation).
3. Channel connections are active and receiving messages.
4. Knowledge bases are searchable.
5. Traces and analytics are recording new data.
6. Scheduled jobs (session cleanup, retention) are running.

chunks.heading: untitled
chunks.text:
# Security & Authentication

Security in the Agent Platform 2.0 is not a feature you enable -- it is a structural property of the system. Every query, every session, and every piece of data is scoped to its owner. This page covers the platform's security architecture, authentication configuration, integration auth patterns, audit logging, and data protection.

**Required role:** Owner or Admin (for workspace security settings)

chunks.heading: Tenant isolation
chunks.text:
### Tenant isolation

A tenant is your organizational boundary in the platform. All resources -- projects, agents, sessions, credentials, and data -- belong to a tenant. The platform enforces tenant isolation at every layer.

**What this means in practice:**

- Every data query includes the tenant identifier as a filter condition. There is no API path or query pattern that can access another tenant's data.
- Cross-tenant access attempts return a "not found" response rather than "access denied." This prevents information leakage -- an attacker cannot even confirm whether a resource exists in another tenant.
- LLM credentials, API keys, and tool bindings are tenant-scoped. Your API keys never touch another tenant's workloads.

Tenant isolation is enforced at the data layer, not the application layer. This means even a bug in business logic cannot bypass isolation boundaries -- the database queries themselves are scoped.

chunks.heading: Project-based resource scoping
chunks.text:
### Project-based resource scoping

Within a tenant, **projects** provide a second level of isolation. Each project is an independent workspace with its own agents, deployments, sessions, and configurations.

Projects are useful for separating concerns within your organization:

- A **customer support** project with triage, billing, and technical agents
- A **sales** project with product recommendation and checkout agents
- An **internal operations** project with HR and IT helpdesk agents

Each project has its own agent definitions, session data, knowledge bases, LLM configuration, tool bindings, and environment variables. A session in one project cannot access data from another project, even within the same tenant. This separation lets you grant different team members access to different projects without risk of cross-project data exposure.

chunks.heading: Access control
chunks.text:
### Access control

The platform implements role-based access control (RBAC) with fine-grained permissions:

| Level   | Scope                                                  |
| ------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| Tenant  | Organization-wide settings, billing, member management |
| Project | Agent definitions, deployments, sessions, tools        |
| User    | Personal API keys, preferences                         |

Permissions are evaluated at the API boundary. Every request checks that the authenticated user has the required permission for the specific resource they are accessing. Insufficient permissions return a "not found" response, consistent with the information-leakage-prevention approach used for tenant isolation.

chunks.heading: Encryption
chunks.text:
### Encryption

All data is encrypted at rest and in transit:

| What                   | How                                                                                            |
| ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Data in transit**    | All communication between services and with external APIs uses TLS encryption                  |
| **Data at rest**       | Sensitive data (credentials, API keys, PII) is encrypted before storage                        |
| **Credential storage** | LLM provider API keys and integration credentials are encrypted using a key management service |
| **Session state**      | Receives additional application-level encryption for sensitive fields                          |

Session-level encryption means even database-level access to raw storage does not expose conversation content without the correct decryption keys. On Enterprise plans with [KMS (Bring Your Own Key)](./billing-quotas-and-operations#kms-bring-your-own-key), your own key material is used.

chunks.heading: Design implications for your agents
chunks.text:
### Design implications for your agents

These security boundaries affect how you design your agent system:

1. **Agents within a project share context.** A supervisor can hand off to any agent in the same project with full session continuity. Design your project boundaries to match trust boundaries.
2. **Cross-project communication requires explicit integration.** If you need agents in different projects to collaborate, use tool-based integrations. This is intentional -- it keeps isolation boundaries clear.
3. **Secrets belong in environment variables, not ABL.** Never hardcode API keys, passwords, or tokens in your agent definitions. Use the platform's environment variable system, which encrypts values and injects them at runtime.
4. **Tool bindings handle auth.** Do not put authentication logic in your agent's instructions or tools. Configure auth at the tool binding level so credentials are managed centrally and rotated without changing agent definitions.

chunks.heading: SSO Configuration
chunks.text:
## SSO Configuration

SSO lets your team authenticate through your organization's identity provider (IdP) instead of managing separate Agent Platform 2.0 passwords.

> **Plan requirement:** SSO is available on Enterprise plans.

### Supported protocols

- **SAML 2.0** -- For identity providers like Okta, Azure AD, OneLogin, and PingIdentity
- **OpenID Connect (OIDC)** -- For providers that support the OIDC protocol, including Azure AD, Auth0, and Google Workspace

### Setting up SAML SSO

1. Go to **Settings > Team > Security**.
2. In the **Single sign-on** section, click **Configure SSO**.
3. Select **SAML 2.0** as the protocol.
4. Copy the **ACS URL** and **Entity ID** displayed -- you need these for your identity provider.
5. In your identity provider's admin console, create a new SAML application using the ACS URL and Entity ID.
6. Copy the **IdP Metadata URL** (or upload the metadata XML) from your identity provider back into the Agent Platform 2.0 configuration.
7. Map the required attributes:
   - `email` (required) -- The user's email address
   - `firstName` (optional) -- The user's first name
   - `lastName` (optional) -- The user's last name
8. Click **Save and test**.
9. Test the connection by signing in through the SSO flow.

### Setting up OIDC SSO

1. Go to **Settings > Team > Security**.
2. In the **Single sign-on** section, click **Configure SSO**.
3. Select **OpenID Connect** as the protocol.
4. Enter the **Discovery URL** (the `.well-known/openid-configuration` endpoint) from your identity provider.
5. Enter the **Client ID** and **Client Secret** from your IdP's application registration.
6. Set the required scopes: `openid`, `email`, `profile`.
7. Click **Save and test**.

### Domain verification

Before enforcing SSO for your workspace, verify ownership of your email domain:

1. In the SSO settings, enter your domain (for example, `yourcompany.com`).
2. Add the provided TXT record to your domain's DNS settings.
3. Click **Verify domain**.
4. Verification typically completes within a few minutes, though DNS propagation can take up to 48 hours.

### Force SSO

Once SSO is configured and your domain is verified, you can require all workspace members to authenticate through SSO:

1. Toggle **Force SSO** to enabled.
2. Confirm the change.

When Force SSO is enabled:

- All workspace members with a verified domain email must authenticate through SSO.
- Password-based login is disabled for those users.
- Members with email addresses outside your verified domain are unaffected.
- The workspace Owner always retains password-based login as a fallback, regardless of the Force SSO setting.

### Google authentication fallback

If you use Google Workspace as your identity provider, team members can authenticate using the "Sign in with Google" button without separate SSO configuration. This is available on all plans and works alongside or independently of SAML/OIDC SSO.

chunks.heading: Multi-Factor Authentication
chunks.text:
## Multi-Factor Authentication

MFA adds a second layer of security by requiring a time-based one-time password (TOTP) in addition to the primary authentication method.

### Enabling MFA for your account

1. Go to your profile settings (click your avatar in the top-right corner).
2. Click **Enable MFA**.
3. Scan the QR code with an authenticator app (Google Authenticator, Authy, 1Password, or any TOTP-compatible app).
4. Enter the six-digit code from your authenticator app to confirm setup.
5. Save the recovery codes displayed on screen in a secure location.

### Recovery codes

When you enable MFA, the platform generates a set of one-time recovery codes. Each recovery code can be used once to sign in if you lose access to your authenticator app.

- Store recovery codes in a secure location (password manager, printed and locked away).
- Each code is single-use. Once used, it cannot be reused.
- You can regenerate recovery codes from your profile settings, which invalidates all previously issued codes.

### MFA enforcement policies

Workspace Owners and Admins can require MFA for the entire workspace:

1. Go to **Settings > Team > Security**.
2. In the **Multi-factor authentication** section, toggle **Require MFA for all members**.
3. Choose a grace period (7, 14, or 30 days) for existing members to set up MFA.

When MFA is enforced:

- New members must configure MFA before they can access workspace resources.
- Existing members see a prompt to set up MFA and have until the grace period expires.
- Members who do not set up MFA within the grace period are locked out until they comply.

> **Tip:** Announce the MFA requirement to your team before enabling enforcement, and set a reasonable grace period to avoid disrupting ongoing work.

chunks.heading: Authentication for Integrations
chunks.text:
## Authentication for Integrations

Agent Platform 2.0 provides multiple ways to authenticate external applications that interact with your deployed agents.

### API keys

API keys are the primary way to authenticate external applications that interact with your deployed agents. You create and manage API keys in Studio, scoped to individual projects.

When your application makes a request to the Runtime API, it includes the API key in the `X-API-Key` header. The Runtime validates the key by:

1. **Hashing the key** and looking up the hash in the database. The original key is never stored -- only its cryptographic hash.
2. **Checking activation status.** Keys can be active or revoked. Revoked keys are rejected immediately.
3. **Verifying project scope.** Each key is tied to a specific project. A key for Project A cannot access agents in Project B.
4. **Validating origins.** If allowed origins are configured for the key, the Runtime checks the request's `Origin` header against the allowlist.

API keys carry permissions that control what the authenticated application can do:

- **Chat** -- Send messages to agents and receive responses
- **Read** -- Access session history, analytics, and agent metadata
- **Admin** -- Manage agents, configurations, and deployments

Follow the principle of least privilege -- a web chat widget only needs Chat permission.

**Key management best practices:**

- Use separate keys for separate environments (development, staging, production).
- Rotate keys periodically. Create a new key, update your application, then revoke the old key.
- Configure allowed origins for browser-based integrations. This prevents your key from being used on unauthorized websites.
- Never embed keys in client-side code that is not origin-restricted.

### SDK tokens

SDK tokens authenticate client-side integrations (web widgets, mobile apps). They are short-lived, limited in scope, and designed to be safe for use in browser-facing code. The browser first exchanges a project-scoped public key on `POST /api/v1/sdk/init`, then uses the issued SDK session token for SDK HTTP routes and the `/ws/sdk` WebSocket. SDK tokens can only create sessions and send messages -- they cannot modify agent definitions or access admin functions.

### Channel authentication

For platform-specific channels (voice, messaging platforms, custom webhooks), the Runtime provides dedicated channel endpoints with their own authentication mechanisms:

| Channel type        | Authentication method                                                         |
| ------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Web SDK**         | Public key exchange on `/api/v1/sdk/init`, then short-lived SDK session token |
| **Voice**           | Session tokens with platform-specific integration                             |
| **Webhooks**        | HMAC signature verification on incoming payloads                              |
| **Custom channels** | API key or JWT-based authentication                                           |

### Service-to-service authentication

When agents call tools or external services, the platform handles authentication on behalf of the agent. Tool bindings can include API key injection, OAuth flows, or custom header-based auth. This means your agent definition declares _what_ tool to call, and the deployment configuration handles _how_ to authenticate.

### Session security

Conversation sessions are protected with several measures:

- Sessions are scoped to a specific project and tenant.
- Session data is encrypted at rest.
- Session timeouts are configurable (idle timeout and absolute timeout).
- Sessions can be explicitly terminated.

chunks.heading: Audit Logging
chunks.text:
## Audit Logging

Audit logs provide a record of sensitive operations performed within your workspace. Use them to investigate security incidents, verify compliance, and understand team activity.

### Viewing audit logs

1. Go to **Settings > Team > Security**.
2. Scroll to the **Audit logs** section, or click the **Audit logs** tab.

The log displays events in reverse chronological order with the following columns:

| Column         | Description                                    |
| -------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| **Timestamp**  | When the action occurred                       |
| **Actor**      | The user who performed the action              |
| **Action**     | The type of operation (see action types below) |
| **Target**     | The resource affected                          |
| **IP address** | The IP address of the request                  |

### Action types

Audit logs capture the following categories of actions:

| Category                | Examples                                                              |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Authentication**      | Login, logout, MFA setup, password reset, API key usage               |
| **Team management**     | Member invited, member removed, role changed, ownership transferred   |
| **Model configuration** | LLM credential added, model configuration changed, credential revoked |
| **Secrets**             | Secret created, secret updated, secret deleted, secret accessed       |
| **Security settings**   | SSO configured, MFA policy changed, domain verified                   |
| **Workspace settings**  | Workspace name changed, retention policy updated                      |
| **Agent operations**    | Agent deployed, agent configuration published                         |
| **Data operations**     | Knowledge base created, documents uploaded, data export requested     |

### Filtering audit logs

Use the filter controls above the log table to narrow results:

- **Actor** -- Filter by the email or name of the user who performed the action
- **Action type** -- Select a specific action category from the dropdown
- **Date range** -- Set start and end dates to scope the time window
- **Target** -- Search for actions on a specific resource by name or ID

### Exporting audit logs

To export audit logs for external analysis or compliance archival:

1. Apply any desired filters.
2. Click **Export CSV**.
3. The platform generates a CSV file with all matching log entries and downloads it to your browser.

The exported CSV includes all columns visible in the log table, plus additional metadata fields useful for compliance auditing.

> **Note:** Audit log retention follows your workspace's data retention policy. On Professional and Enterprise plans, logs are retained for at least 90 days. Contact support if you need extended retention.

chunks.heading: Data Protection & Compliance
chunks.text:
## Data Protection & Compliance

### Data retention

Each tenant configures a retention period for session data. After the retention window closes, session data is permanently deleted. This applies to:

- Conversation histories
- Session variables and gathered data
- Trace events and execution logs
- Cold-stored session snapshots

Retention is enforced automatically. You do not need to write cleanup logic -- the platform handles deletion on schedule.

### Right to erasure

The platform supports data deletion requests at the user level. When a deletion request is processed, all data associated with that user -- across all sessions and projects within the tenant -- is removed. This cascade covers conversation history, gathered data, persistent memory facts, and any derived analytics.

### PII handling

Agents that handle personally identifiable information (PII) can be configured with redaction policies. The platform can detect and tokenize PII in both input and output, preventing sensitive data from reaching LLM providers or appearing in logs.

- **PII detection** -- Configurable patterns detect PII in user messages and agent responses.
- **PII redaction** -- Detected PII can be automatically redacted before storage or display.
- **PII audit logging** -- Access to PII-containing data is logged for compliance auditing.
- **Output filtering** -- Output guardrails can filter PII from agent responses before delivery.

PII redaction is configurable per environment. You might disable it in development for easier debugging while enforcing it in production.

### LLM provider data handling

When your agent calls an LLM provider, the conversation content is sent to that provider's API. Each provider has its own data handling policies:

- **Anthropic** -- Does not use API data for training by default
- **OpenAI** -- Enterprise and API accounts have data usage opt-out
- **Azure OpenAI** -- Data stays within your Azure tenant
- **AWS Bedrock** -- Data stays within your AWS account

You choose which provider to use, and you configure the credentials. The platform routes requests to the provider you select.

### Data residency

Your data stays within the infrastructure you deploy to. For cloud deployments, you choose the region. The platform does not replicate data across regions unless you configure it.

chunks.heading: Security Best Practices
chunks.text:
## Security Best Practices

- **Enable MFA enforcement** for all workspace members, especially if your workspace contains production agent configurations or customer data.
- **Configure SSO** to centralize authentication and take advantage of your organization's identity governance policies.
- **Review audit logs regularly** -- Schedule a weekly review of authentication-related events to catch suspicious activity early.
- **Use the principle of least privilege** -- Assign members the minimum role they need. Most team members need Member or Viewer access, not Admin.
- **Rotate secrets periodically** -- See [Workspace Configuration](./workspace-configuration) for guidance on secret rotation.

chunks.heading: untitled
chunks.text:
# Workspace & Team Management

Your workspace is the central hub for managing agents, team members, AI models, and billing within the Agent Platform 2.0. Every resource you create lives inside a workspace, and every team member you invite joins through one. This page covers workspace structure, team management, and multi-workspace organizations.

chunks.heading: Resource hierarchy
chunks.text:
### Resource hierarchy The Agent Platform 2.0 organizes resources in a three-level hierarchy: ```mermaid graph TD Org["Organization
optional"] W["Workspace"] P["Project"] A["Agents"] KB["Knowledge bases"] T["Tools"] EV["Environment variables"] S["Sessions"] Org --> W W --> P P --> A P --> KB P --> T P --> EV P --> S ``` **Workspace** is the primary unit of tenancy. It contains your team, AI model configurations, secrets, connectors, guardrails, and analytics data. Think of it as your team's dedicated environment within the platform. Key characteristics: - **Isolated by default** -- Data in one workspace is never accessible from another workspace. - **Owns LLM policies** -- Token budgets, allowed providers, and rate limits are set at the workspace level. - **Manages team access** -- Members are invited to and managed within a workspace. - **Controls feature availability** -- Your workspace's plan tier determines which features are accessible. **Project** groups related agents and their supporting resources (knowledge bases, tools, environment variables) for a specific use case. A workspace can contain many projects. - Each project inherits the workspace's AI model configuration but can override model tier assignments. - Project members are a subset of workspace members, with project-specific roles. - Environment variables and secrets can be scoped to individual projects and environments (development, staging, production).

chunks.heading: Role hierarchy
chunks.text:
### Role hierarchy

The Agent Platform 2.0 uses a hierarchical role-based access control (RBAC) system. Each role inherits all permissions from the roles below it.

Canonical engineering detail for role behavior lives in `docs/features/workspace-sharing.md` (workspace roles) and `docs/features/custom-project-roles.md` (project and custom roles). The tables below summarize the current product behavior.

#### Workspace roles

| Role         | Description                                               | Key permissions                                                                         |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Owner**    | Full control over the workspace. One owner per workspace. | Transfer ownership, delete workspace, manage billing, all admin operations              |
| **Admin**    | Manages team members, models, and workspace settings.     | Invite/remove members, configure models, manage secrets and connectors, view audit logs |
| **Operator** | Manages agent deployments and monitors production.        | Deploy agents, view analytics, manage environment variables, view sessions              |
| **Member**   | Builds and tests agents within assigned projects.         | Create/edit agents, run tests, manage project-level resources                           |
| **Viewer**   | Read-only access to workspace resources.                  | View agents, view project configurations, view analytics dashboards                     |

#### Project roles

Projects have their own role assignments that further restrict or specialize access within the project:

| Role          | Description                                                              |
| ------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Admin**     | Full control over the project's resources and settings                   |
| **Developer** | Create and modify agents, tools, workflows, and other project assets     |
| **Tester**    | Read project resources, run simulations/evaluations, and verify behavior |
| **Viewer**    | Read-only access to the project                                          |

> **Note:** Workspace Owners and Admins have workspace-wide authority and do not need explicit project membership to administer all projects. Non-admin workspace members need explicit project membership for project-scoped access.

> **Project ownership:** When a workspace member creates a project, they become that project's owner. Project owners can add existing workspace members to the project from **Settings > Members** even if they are not workspace admins.

#### Custom roles

Workspace Owners and Admins can define tenant-scoped custom role definitions with specific permission sets. Manage them from **Settings > Team > Custom Roles**. These definitions feed the project-level custom role model described in `docs/features/custom-project-roles.md`.

chunks.heading: Feature gating and plan tiers
chunks.text:
### Feature gating and plan tiers

Not all workspace features are available on every plan. The Agent Platform 2.0 offers tiered plans that unlock progressively more capabilities:

| Feature                  | Starter | Professional  | Enterprise    |
| ------------------------ | ------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| Core agent builder       | Yes     | Yes           | Yes           |
| Team members             | Up to 5 | Up to 25      | Unlimited     |
| Projects                 | Up to 3 | Up to 20      | Unlimited     |
| LLM providers            | 2       | All supported | All supported |
| Environment variables    | Yes     | Yes           | Yes           |
| Connectors               | --      | Yes           | Yes           |
| Advanced analytics       | --      | Yes           | Yes           |
| KMS (Bring Your Own Key) | --      | --            | Yes           |
| SSO (SAML/OIDC)          | --      | --            | Yes           |
| Audit log export         | --      | Yes           | Yes           |
| Workspace guardrails     | --      | Yes           | Yes           |

Features not available on your current plan appear greyed out in the Studio sidebar with an "Upgrade to unlock" tooltip. Contact your account team or visit **Settings > Billing** to explore plan options.

chunks.heading: Navigating workspace settings
chunks.text:
### Navigating workspace settings

Access workspace administration through the **Settings** icon in the Studio sidebar. The admin panel is organized into four sections:

- **Team** -- Members, security, KMS, environment variables
- **AI configuration** -- LLM providers, Architect settings, voice services, guardrails
- **Analytics** -- Agent performance, session explorer, trace viewer
- **Account** -- Secrets, billing and usage, connectors

> **Tip:** Bookmark the admin panel pages you visit frequently. Each admin page has a stable URL you can share with other admins on your team.

> **Access note:** Only workspace Owners and Admins see workspace administration pages in Studio. Operators, Members, and Viewers collaborate through project-level settings instead.

chunks.heading: Team Management
chunks.text:
## Team Management

Manage who has access to your workspace by inviting team members, assigning roles, and controlling permissions. Only workspace Owners and Admins can manage team membership.

chunks.heading: Viewing current members
chunks.text:
### Viewing current members

Navigate to **Settings > Team > Members** to see all current workspace members. The members list shows:

- **Name and email** -- The member's display name and login email
- **Role** -- Their current workspace role (Owner, Admin, Operator, Member, or Viewer)
- **Status** -- Whether the member is active, suspended, locked, or deactivated
- **Joined date** -- When the member accepted their invitation

Use the search bar to filter members by name or email.

chunks.heading: Inviting members
chunks.text:
### Inviting members

To invite a new team member:

1. Go to **Settings > Team > Members**.
2. Click **Invite member**.
3. Enter the invitee's email address.
4. Select a role from the dropdown (Admin, Operator, Member, or Viewer).
5. Click **Send invitation**.

The invitee receives an email with a unique invitation link. The invitation includes the workspace name, assigned role, and a link to accept and join.

#### Invitation expiry

Invitations expire automatically after 7 days. If an invitation expires before the recipient accepts it:

- The expired invitation is cleaned up automatically.
- You can resend a new invitation from the pending invitations list.

#### Pending invitations

The members page shows a separate section for pending invitations. From here you can:

- **Resend** -- Send a fresh invitation email (generates a new token and resets the expiry).
- **Revoke** -- Cancel a pending invitation so the link can no longer be used.

> **Tip:** If a team member reports they did not receive the invitation email, check the pending invitations list first. You can resend it without revoking the original.

chunks.heading: Managing roles and permissions
chunks.text:
### Managing roles and permissions

#### Changing a member's role

1. Find the member in the members list.
2. Click the role dropdown next to their name.
3. Select the new role.
4. Confirm the change.

Role changes take effect immediately. The member's permissions update on their next page load or API call.

#### Role hierarchy enforcement

The platform enforces strict role hierarchy rules:

- You cannot assign a role higher than your own. An Admin cannot promote another member to Owner.
- Only the workspace Owner can promote a member to Admin.
- Only the workspace Owner can transfer ownership (see below).
- You cannot demote yourself. To step down, ask another Owner or Admin to change your role.

#### Creating custom roles

For fine-grained access control, create tenant-scoped custom role definitions:

1. Go to **Settings > Team > Custom Roles**.
2. Click **Create role**.
3. Enter a role name and description.
4. Toggle the permissions you want to grant.
5. Click **Save**.

These custom roles are tenant-scoped definitions. Built-in workspace role assignment still happens on the Members page, while project-level custom-role assignment is governed by the Custom Project Roles flow.

Permission strings follow the format `resource:action` (for example, `agent:read`, `workflow:*`, `secret:read`). The full registry lives in `packages/shared-auth/src/rbac/role-permissions.ts`.

chunks.heading: Removing members
chunks.text:
### Removing members

To remove a member from the workspace:

1. Find the member in the members list.
2. Click the three-dot menu next to their row.
3. Select **Remove member**.
4. Confirm the removal.

When a member is removed:
